title: A Global Power
overview:
During the 18th and 19th centuries, Britain transformed from a constitutional monarchy into a global superpower. This period saw the Bill of Rights limiting the monarch's power, the Act of Union creating Great Britain, the Enlightenment fostering new ideas, the Industrial Revolution changing how people lived and worked, the expansion and eventual abolition of the slave trade, and the growth of the British Empire to become the largest the world has ever seen.

This lesson covers Britain's transformation into a global power, from constitutional changes and industrial development to democratic reforms and imperial expansion.

study_sections.heading: Constitutional Monarchy - The Bill of Rights 1689
study_sections.content:
At the coronation of William and Mary, a Declaration of Rights was read. This confirmed that the king would no longer be able to raise taxes or administer justice without agreement from Parliament. The balance of power between monarch and Parliament had now permanently changed.

The Bill of Rights, 1689, confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king's power. Parliament took control of who could be monarch and declared that the king or queen must be a Protestant. A new Parliament had to be elected at least every three years (later this became seven years and now it is five years). Every year the monarch had to ask Parliament to renew funding for the army and the navy.

These changes meant that the monarch needed to have advisers, or ministers, who would be able to ensure a majority of votes in the House of Commons and the House of Lords. There were two main groups in Parliament, known as the Whigs and the Tories. (The modern Conservative Party is still sometimes referred to as the Tories.) This was the beginning of party politics.

From 1695, newspapers were allowed to operate without a government licence. This was an important development for a free press. The laws passed after the Glorious Revolution are the beginning of what is called 'constitutional monarchy'. The monarch remained very important but was no longer able to insist on particular policies if Parliament did not agree.

study_sections.heading: The Act of Union 1707 - Creating Great Britain
study_sections.content:
William and Mary's successor, Queen Anne, had no surviving children. This created uncertainty over the succession in England, Wales and Ireland and in Scotland. The Act of Union, known as the Treaty of Union in Scotland, was therefore agreed in 1707, creating the Kingdom of Great Britain.

Although Scotland was no longer an independent country, it kept its own legal and education systems and Presbyterian Church.

study_sections.heading: The Prime Minister
study_sections.content:
When Queen Anne died in 1714, Parliament chose a German, George I, to be the next king, because he was Anne's nearest Protestant relative. An attempt by Scottish Jacobites to put James II's son on the throne instead was quickly defeated.

George I did not speak very good English and this increased his need to rely on his ministers. The most important minister in Parliament became known as the Prime Minister. The first man to be called this was Sir Robert Walpole, who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

study_sections.heading: The Rebellion of the Clans - Bonnie Prince Charlie
study_sections.content:
In 1745 there was another attempt to put a Stuart king back on the throne in place of George I's son, George II. Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie), the grandson of James II, landed in Scotland. He was supported by clansmen from the Scottish highlands and raised an army. Charles initially had some successes but was defeated by George II's army at the Battle of Culloden in 1746. Charles escaped back to Europe.

The clans lost a lot of their power and influence after Culloden. Chieftains became landlords if they had the favour of the English king, and clansmen became tenants who had to pay for the land they used.

A process began which became known as the 'Highland Clearances'. Many Scottish landlords destroyed individual small farms (known as 'crofts') to make space for large flocks of sheep and cattle. Evictions became very common in the early 19th century. Many Scottish people left for North America at this time.

study_sections.heading: The Enlightenment
study_sections.content:
During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called 'the Enlightenment'. Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish.

Adam Smith developed ideas about economics which are still referred to today. David Hume's ideas about human nature continue to influence philosophers. Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt's work on steam power, helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

One of the most important principles of the Enlightenment was that everyone should have the right to their own political and religious beliefs and that the state should not try to dictate to them. This continues to be an important principle in the UK today.

study_sections.heading: The Industrial Revolution
study_sections.content:
Before the 18th century, agriculture was the biggest source of employment in Britain. There were many cottage industries, where people worked from home to produce goods such as cloth and lace.

The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries. Britain was the first country to industrialise on a large scale. It happened because of the development of machinery and the use of steam power. Agriculture and the manufacturing of goods became mechanised. This made things more efficient and increased production.

Coal and other raw materials were needed to power the new factories. Many people moved from the countryside and started working in the mining and manufacturing industries. The development of the Bessemer process for the mass production of steel led to the development of the shipbuilding industry and the railways. Manufacturing jobs became the main source of employment in Britain.

Richard Arkwright (1732-92) improved textile machinery and developed efficient horse-driven and later steam-powered spinning mills. Better transport links were needed, so canals were built to link factories to towns, cities and ports.

Working conditions during the Industrial Revolution were very poor. There were no laws to protect employees, who were often forced to work long hours in dangerous situations. Children also worked and were treated in the same way as adults, sometimes even more harshly.

study_sections.heading: The Slave Trade and Its Abolition
study_sections.content:
Britain's commercial expansion and prosperity was sustained in part by the booming slave trade. While slavery was illegal within Britain itself, by the 18th century it was a fully established overseas industry, dominated by Britain and the American colonies.

Slaves came primarily from West Africa. Travelling on British ships in horrible conditions, they were taken to America and the Caribbean, where they were made to work on tobacco and sugar plantations. The living and working conditions for slaves were very bad. Many slaves tried to escape and others revolted against their owners.

However, there were people in Britain who opposed the slave trade. The first formal anti-slavery groups were set up by the Quakers in the late 1700s, and they petitioned Parliament to ban the practice. William Wilberforce, an evangelical Christian and a member of Parliament, also played an important part in changing the law. Along with other abolitionists, he succeeded in turning public opinion against the slave trade.

In 1807, it became illegal to trade slaves in British ships or from British ports, and in 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire. The Royal Navy stopped slave ships from other countries, freed the slaves and punished the slave traders.

study_sections.heading: The American War of Independence
study_sections.content:
By the 1760s, there were substantial British colonies in North America. The colonies were wealthy and largely in control of their own affairs. The British government wanted to tax the colonies. The colonists saw this as an attack on their freedom and said there should be 'no taxation without representation' in the British Parliament.

Fighting broke out between the colonists and the British forces. In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments. The colonists eventually defeated the British army and Britain recognised the colonies' independence in 1783.

study_sections.heading: War with France - Trafalgar and Waterloo
study_sections.content:
During the 18th century, Britain fought a number of wars with France. In 1789, there was a revolution in France and the new French government soon declared war on Britain. Napoleon, who became Emperor of France, continued the war.

Britain's navy fought against combined French and Spanish fleets, winning the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805. Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet at Trafalgar and was killed in the battle. Nelson's Column in Trafalgar Square, London, is a monument to him. His ship, HMS Victory, can be visited in Portsmouth.

The British army also fought against the French. In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo. Wellington was known as the Iron Duke and later became Prime Minister.

study_sections.heading: The Act of Union 1800 - The United Kingdom
study_sections.content:
Although Ireland had had the same monarch as England and Wales since Henry VIII, it had remained a separate country. In 1801, Ireland became unified with England, Scotland and Wales after the Act of Union of 1800. This created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

One symbol of this union was a new version of the official flag, the Union Flag (often called the Union Jack). The flag combined crosses associated with England, Scotland and Ireland:
• The cross of St George (England): red cross on white ground
• The cross of St Andrew (Scotland): diagonal white cross on blue ground
• The cross of St Patrick (Ireland): diagonal red cross on white ground

The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because when the first Union Flag was created in 1606, Wales was already united with England.

study_sections.heading: The Victorian Age and the British Empire
study_sections.content:
In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18. She reigned until 1901, almost 64 years - the longest reign of any British monarch at that time. Her reign is known as the Victorian Age. It was a time when Britain increased in power and influence abroad.

During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa. It became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

Britain continued to be a great trading nation. The government began to promote policies of free trade, abolishing taxes on imported goods. The Corn Laws were repealed in 1846, which had prevented the import of cheap grain. These reforms helped the development of British industry.

Working conditions in factories gradually became better. In 1847, the number of hours that women and children could work was limited by law to 10 hours per day. Better housing began to be built for workers.

Isambard Kingdom Brunel (1806-59) was an engineer who built tunnels, bridges, railway lines and ships. He built the Great Western Railway, the first major railway in Britain. British industry led the world in the 19th century. The UK produced more than half of the world's iron, coal and cotton cloth.

study_sections.heading: The Right to Vote
study_sections.content:
As the middle classes grew in influence, they began to demand more political power. The Reform Act of 1832 greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote. It also abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs and gave more parliamentary seats to towns and cities. However, voting was still based on ownership of property, so working class people still could not vote.

Campaigners called the Chartists presented petitions to Parliament demanding the vote for the working classes. In 1867 there was another Reform Act, which created many more urban seats and reduced the amount of property needed to vote. However, the majority of men still did not have the right to vote and no women could vote.

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, an increasing number of women campaigned for greater rights and, in particular, the right to vote. They formed the women's suffrage movement and became known as 'suffragettes'. Emmeline Pankhurst (1858-1928) helped found the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU) in 1903. The group used civil disobedience, including chaining themselves to railings, smashing windows and committing arson. In 1918, women over the age of 30 were given voting rights, partly in recognition of women's contribution to the war effort. In 1928, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

memory_hook: Remember Britain as a global power with 'RISE': Rights established (Bill of Rights 1689), Industrial Revolution (first to industrialise), Slavery abolished (1807 trade, 1833 completely), Empire expanded (largest ever, 400 million people). Key dates: 1707 Act of Union, 1746 Culloden, 1776 American independence, 1805 Trafalgar, 1815 Waterloo, 1833 slavery abolished, 1867 Reform Act.

questions.prompt: When was the Bill of Rights?
questions.explanation: The Bill of Rights was passed in 1689, confirming Parliament's rights and limiting the monarch's power.

questions.prompt: What did the Bill of Rights 1689 do?
options: Confirmed the rights of Parliament and limits of the king's power
Gave everyone the right to vote
questions.explanation: The Bill of Rights permanently changed the balance of power between monarch and Parliament.

questions.prompt: Must the monarch be a Protestant?
questions.explanation: Parliament took control of who could be monarch and declared they must be a Protestant.

questions.prompt: When could newspapers operate without a government licence?
questions.explanation: From 1695, newspapers were allowed to operate without a government licence, developing a free press.

questions.prompt: What were the two main political groups?
questions.explanation: The Whigs and Tories were the two main groups in Parliament, beginning party politics.

questions.prompt: When was the Act of Union creating Great Britain?
questions.explanation: The Act of Union 1707 created the Kingdom of Great Britain from England, Scotland and Wales.

questions.prompt: What did Scotland keep after the Act of Union 1707?
options: Its own legal and education systems and Presbyterian Church
questions.explanation: Scotland kept its legal and education systems and Presbyterian Church.

questions.prompt: Who was the first Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: Sir Robert Walpole was the first man to be called Prime Minister (1721-1742).

questions.prompt: When was Sir Robert Walpole Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: Walpole was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

questions.prompt: Who was Bonnie Prince Charlie?
options: Charles Edward Stuart, grandson of James II
questions.explanation: Bonnie Prince Charlie (Charles Edward Stuart) was the grandson of James II.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of Culloden?
questions.explanation: Bonnie Prince Charlie was defeated at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.

questions.prompt: What were the Highland Clearances?
options: Destruction of small farms to make space for sheep and cattle
questions.explanation: Many landlords destroyed crofts to make space for livestock, causing evictions.

questions.prompt: Which Enlightenment thinker developed ideas about economics?
questions.explanation: Adam Smith developed ideas about economics that are still referred to today.

questions.prompt: Which country was first to industrialise on a large scale?
questions.explanation: Britain was the first country to industrialise on a large scale.

questions.prompt: When became illegal to trade slaves in British ships?
questions.explanation: In 1807, it became illegal to trade slaves in British ships or from British ports.

questions.prompt: When was the Emancipation Act?
questions.explanation: In 1833, the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

questions.prompt: Who played an important part in abolishing slavery?
questions.explanation: William Wilberforce, an MP, succeeded in turning public opinion against the slave trade.

questions.prompt: When did American colonies declare independence?
questions.explanation: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence.

questions.prompt: When did Britain recognise American independence?
questions.explanation: Britain recognised the colonies' independence in 1783.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of Trafalgar?
questions.explanation: Britain won the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805.

questions.prompt: Who was killed at the Battle of Trafalgar?
questions.explanation: Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet and was killed in the battle.

questions.prompt: When was the Battle of Waterloo?
questions.explanation: The French Wars ended in 1815 with Napoleon's defeat at Waterloo.

questions.prompt: Who defeated Napoleon at Waterloo?
questions.explanation: The Duke of Wellington defeated Napoleon at the Battle of Waterloo.

questions.prompt: When was the Act of Union with Ireland?
questions.explanation: The Act of Union 1800 created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland (unified in 1801).

questions.prompt: What does the Union Flag combine?
options: Crosses of St George, St Andrew and St Patrick
Crosses of all four UK countries
questions.explanation: The Union Flag combines crosses of England, Scotland and Ireland.

questions.prompt: When did Queen Victoria become queen?
questions.explanation: Queen Victoria became queen in 1837 at the age of 18.

questions.prompt: How long did Queen Victoria reign?
questions.explanation: Victoria reigned from 1837 to 1901, almost 64 years.

questions.prompt: How large was the British Empire's population?
questions.explanation: The British Empire had an estimated population of more than 400 million people.

questions.prompt: When were the Corn Laws repealed?
questions.explanation: The Corn Laws were repealed in 1846, allowing import of cheap grain.

questions.prompt: Who built the Great Western Railway?
questions.explanation: Brunel built the Great Western Railway, the first major railway in Britain.

questions.prompt: When was the Reform Act that increased voting rights?
questions.explanation: The Reform Act of 1832 greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote.

questions.prompt: What were the Chartists?
options: Campaigners demanding the vote for working classes
questions.explanation: The Chartists presented petitions demanding voting rights for the working classes.

questions.prompt: When was the second Reform Act?
questions.explanation: The Reform Act of 1867 created more urban seats and reduced property requirements.

questions.prompt: Who founded the WSPU in 1903?
questions.explanation: Emmeline Pankhurst helped found the Women's Social and Political Union in 1903.

questions.prompt: When did women over 30 get the vote?
questions.explanation: In 1918, women over 30 were given voting rights and the right to stand for Parliament.

questions.prompt: When did women get the vote at age 21?
questions.explanation: In 1928, women were given the right to vote at age 21, the same as men.

flashcards: What did Bill of Rights do?
Confirmed Parliament's rights, limited monarch's power
Act of Union creating Great Britain?
Own legal system, education, Presbyterian Church
Charles Edward Stuart, James II's grandson
Destruction of small farms for sheep/cattle

supportive_messages.high_score: Outstanding! You know Britain's rise to global power brilliantly! 🌟
supportive_messages.medium_score: Good work! Review the key dates - there are many important ones! 📚
